title: Prison Rape Prevention Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill sets new rules for federal prisons about how to treat prisoners based on their biological sex. It says that prisoners must be housed and transported only with other prisoners who have the same biological sex they were born with. The bill also stops federal prisons from providing medical treatments that help people change their gender. There are a few exceptions for people with certain medical conditions. The bill explains what biological sex, gender, and gender-related medical treatment mean in the law.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Prison Rape Prevention Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Rules for Housing or Transportation Based on Gender; Limitation on Gender-Affirming Care
sections.text:
This section changes the federal law about prisons. It adds new rules to section three thousand six hundred twenty one of title eighteen of the United States Code. The new rules say that the Bureau of Prisons (the government agency that runs federal prisons) must follow three main requirements. First, prisoners must be housed only with other prisoners of the same biological sex. This means a prisoner can only be placed in cells or living areas with prisoners who have the same biological sex as them during their entire prison sentence. Second, when prisoners are moved from one place to another with other prisoners, they can only travel with prisoners of the same biological sex. Third, the Bureau of Prisons cannot provide or pay for any gender-related medical treatment for prisoners.

The section also includes definitions of important terms. Biological sex means the scientific determination of whether someone is male or female. A female is defined as someone who naturally has or would have the reproductive system that produces, moves, and uses eggs for creating babies. A male is defined as someone who naturally has or would have the reproductive system that produces, moves, and uses sperm for creating babies. These definitions account for unusual cases caused by genetic problems or accidents.

Gender is defined as males, females, or the natural differences between males and females. It is treated as the same thing as biological sex. The definition specifically says gender does not mean gender identity, experienced gender, gender expression, or gender roles.

Gender-related medical treatment is defined as medical care given to help someone who believes their gender or sex is different from what it actually is. For females, this includes many surgical procedures like removing reproductive organs, creating male-appearing genitals, removing breasts, and changing vocal cords. It also includes taking testosterone or other male hormones and taking puberty blockers that stop female development. For males, this includes surgical procedures like removing male genitals, creating female-appearing genitals, breast implants, and facial surgeries to look more female. It also includes taking estrogen and puberty blockers that stop male development. The prison director can identify other treatments that fit this definition.

The definition does not include medical treatment for certain conditions. These exceptions are treatment for disorders of sex development that a doctor diagnoses through genetic or blood tests, treatment for people born with unclear biological sex characteristics including certain chromosome patterns, and treatment for infections, injuries, diseases, or disorders caused by previous gender-related medical treatment.
